title: Does the hadith "la adwa" mean Islam denies that diseases are contagious?
stanceSummary: The hadith 'la adwa' (there is no adwa) does not deny the physical transmission of disease. It negates the pre-Islamic pagan superstition that merely being in the presence of a sick person would supernaturally cause illness. Other authentic hadith prove the Prophet ﷺ fully accepted physical transmission: he told healthy people not to enter plague-ridden lands, ordered sick animals to water separately from healthy ones, and said to flee from a leper as from a lion. Modern scholars are essentially unanimous on this understanding.
alternateQuestions: Does Islam say diseases cannot spread from person to person?
What does the hadith "la adwa" mean?
Is it Islamic to believe diseases are not contagious?
How do I respond when non-Muslims say Islam denies contagion?
Did the Prophet say there is no such thing as disease transmission?
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2020 Ask Shaykh YQ video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oZw8bX1nKbQ) (starting at [0:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oZw8bX1nKbQ&t=0s)), Yasir Qadhi responds to a sister who saw a non-Muslim website claiming Islam denies that diseases are contagious, citing a hadith in Sahih Bukhari.

- The hadith *"la adwa, wala tiyara, wala hama, wala safar"* negates **four pre-Islamic superstitions** — all of them supernatural/pagan notions
- **Adwa** in its original meaning was the superstitious belief that a sick person would *automatically* make a nearby healthy person sick through some supernatural mechanism
- Some early scholars (Ibn Qutayba, etc.) did interpret this as denying physical transmission — but Yasir Qadhi notes they were products of their time without knowledge of microbes
- The **correct interpretation**, supported by multiple other authentic hadith, is that the Prophet ﷺ fully accepted natural disease transmission
- Modern scholars are essentially **unanimous** on this interpretation

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Hadith in Question — [0:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oZw8bX1nKbQ&t=0s)

A sister writes: a non-Muslim website claimed that Islam doesn't believe diseases are transferable and quoted a hadith from Sahih Bukhari. She is confused.

This is why scholars say: be careful what you read online. Sometimes an article is designed to ridicule Islam and will pick out a hadith without context. The hadith in question is:

*"La adwa, wala tiyara, wala hama, wala safar"* — The Prophet ﷺ negated four things: adwa, tiyara (bad omens from birds), hama (omen from an owl), and safar (the month of Safar being unlucky).

Note what the other three items are: they are all **pagan supernatural superstitions** — bad omens from seeing a particular bird, believing an owl predicts death, and believing a particular month is cursed. All four of these are pre-Islamic pagan notions.

### What "Adwa" Means — [1:30](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oZw8bX1nKbQ&t=90s)

"Adwa" comes from the same root as "adu" (enemy) — to attack or assault. Adwa was the pre-Islamic Arab belief that a sick person would **automatically and supernaturally** make a nearby healthy person sick, simply by their presence. The Prophet ﷺ negated this superstitious idea.

Some early great scholars — including Ibn Qutayba and al-Baji — did interpret this to mean there is no physical transmission of disease whatsoever. They were great scholars, but they were also products of a time before germ theory, before microscopes, and before anyone understood viruses. They did not know what microbes were. Their interpretation reflected their cultural and scientific limitations.

### The Counter-Evidence from the Same Sunnah — [2:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oZw8bX1nKbQ&t=120s)

If the hadith truly meant there is no physical transmission of disease, how do we explain these other sahih hadith?

1. **Don't enter a plague-ridden land** (Sahih Bukhari and Muslim): *"If you hear of a plague in a land, do not enter it."* Why would this matter if healthy people can't catch it?

2. **Sick camels must drink separately** (Abu Dawud): The Prophet ﷺ instructed that sick animals and healthy animals should not water at the same time. There is a clear causal relationship being acknowledged here.

3. **Flee from the leper as you flee from a lion** (Ibn Majah): Leprosy was one of the most contagious diseases known. The Prophet ﷺ told people to stay away from lepers — exactly because physical proximity increases the risk of transmission.

These three hadith, all authenticated, clearly demonstrate that the Prophet ﷺ acknowledged natural disease transmission. This obliges us to interpret *"la adwa"* as negating the **supernatural** superstition, not the natural reality of contagion.

### An Important Lesson — [3:00](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oZw8bX1nKbQ&t=180s)

This example also teaches us something important: it is permissible — and sometimes necessary — to revisit positions taken by earlier scholars in light of modern knowledge. Our great ulama were not infallible. They were products of their time. They did not know what we know about microbes and viruses.

The position that diseases do not transmit physically from person to person simply cannot be maintained in light of what modern science has established. And the good news is: the Sunnah itself supports the correct position. The Prophet ﷺ was fully aware that sick and healthy animals should be separated, that a leper should be avoided, and that one should not walk into a plague zone.

This is why today, essentially every scholar of our times holds the correct position: the hadith negates supernatural pagan beliefs about disease, not the reality of natural contagion. And Allah knows best.
